common.metadata.title: Metro Typing | Seoul Subway Typing Game
common.metadata.description: Metro Typing is a game where you type subway station names quickly and accurately to finish a line. Pick a Seoul Metro line and challenge your typing record.

landing.hero.headline: The headless engine for your next SaaS.
landing.hero.subheadline: Auth, payments, subscriptions, credits, RBAC, CMS, and i18n — all pre-wired. Just build your product on top.
landing.features.title: Everything you need to ship
landing.features.description: Pre-wired business logic so you can focus on building your product.
landing.features.auth.title: Authentication
landing.features.auth.description: Email/password, Google, and GitHub OAuth — ready out of the box with session management and account linking.
landing.features.payment.title: Payments
landing.features.payment.description: Stripe, PayPal, Alipay, WeChat Pay — checkout, subscriptions, webhooks, and order management.
landing.features.rbac.title: Roles & Permissions
landing.features.rbac.description: Fine-grained RBAC with wildcard matching. Full admin panel for managing users, roles, and permissions.
landing.features.i18n.title: Internationalization
landing.features.i18n.description: English and Chinese built-in with locale-aware routing. Easy to extend with additional languages.
landing.features.cms.title: Content Management
landing.features.cms.description: Categories and posts with full CRUD, status management, and admin panel — build your blog or docs.
landing.features.credits.title: Credit System
landing.features.credits.description: FIFO consumption, expiration, auto-grant on signup, and revocation — ready for usage-based billing.
landing.pricing.title: Pricing
landing.pricing.description: Choose the plan that works for you.
landing.faq.title: Frequently asked questions
landing.faq.description: Everything you need to know before getting started.
landing.faq.stack.question: What's the tech stack?
landing.faq.stack.answer: Next.js 16 with the App Router, React 19, TypeScript strict mode, Tailwind CSS 4, shadcn/ui, Drizzle ORM, and better-auth. Every piece of business logic is inlined — no external packages for payment, email, storage, or AI.
landing.faq.payment.question: Which payment providers are supported?
landing.faq.payment.answer: Stripe, PayPal, Creem, Alipay, and WeChat Pay out of the box. The PaymentManager abstraction makes it straightforward to add more providers by implementing a single interface.
landing.faq.database.question: Which databases can I use?
landing.faq.database.answer: Drizzle ORM handles PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, Turso, and Cloudflare D1. Switch providers by editing DATABASE_PROVIDER in your .env — the schema templates and drizzle config adapt automatically.
landing.faq.customize.question: How customizable is the landing page?
landing.faq.customize.answer: The src/blocks/ directory ships with demo sections — they're designed to be rewritten per project. The durable primitives live in src/components/, so your rebrand touches blocks and translations, not the chassis.
landing.faq.license.question: Can I use this for commercial projects?
landing.faq.license.answer: Yes. ShipAny Next is built for shipping production SaaS products. You own the code after cloning — no per-seat fees, no runtime dependencies on our infrastructure.
landing.cta.headline: Ready to ship your SaaS?
landing.cta.subheadline: Stop wiring auth, payments, and RBAC from scratch. Start with ShipAny Next and build what actually makes your product unique.
landing.footer.tagline: Practice Hangul typing with subway station names — and have fun.

landing.seo.about.title: What is Metro Typing?
landing.seo.about.p1: Metro Typing is a game where typing a subway station name moves the train to the next stop. It is different from a typical typing test of random words. You follow a real line order, recall the next station, and when you get it right the train advances one stop. Mistakes keep you in place until you type it correctly. You can play Metro Typing right at the top of the page — no install needed.
landing.seo.about.p2: When a run ends, you see finish time, typing speed, and accuracy together. The numbers show where you stumbled, so you know what to watch on the next try. Metro Typing looks like a subway-station typing game, but the goal is simple: push the line you chose all the way to the end.
landing.seo.about.p3: The subway typing game runs in the browser. It fits a short break or a quick round before commuting, without feeling like a heavy practice mode. Finishing a line by typing station names gives a clear goal, so you want to push through more than plain letter drills. After a few Metro Typing rounds, you notice which familiar stops on your usual line come to mind slower than you expect.
landing.seo.howto.title: How to play Metro Typing
landing.seo.howto.p1: First pick the subway line you want to play. A line you ride often helps the next station come to mind so your rhythm stays smooth. If you are new, start with a shorter stretch that has fewer stations. For a first Seoul Metro typing run, a short section is easier than a long line.
landing.seo.howto.p2: Choose a direction. Upbound and downbound change the station order. Picking the side you actually commute on makes memory and typing click together. Then type the current station name in the input. Spelling must be correct for the train to move. Typos stop you in place, so checking letter by letter is often faster than rushing.
landing.seo.howto.p3: Finish the line to see completion time and typing speed on the results screen. Repeat the same line two or three times and your frequent mistakes become obvious. Then drill just that stretch to rebuild muscle memory. The Metro Typing flow is simple: pick, type, and review where you failed.
landing.seo.howto.p4: One tip: say the next station out loud before you type. When your hands lead, typos rise; when words lead, accuracy often steadies. Sticking to one line and one direction makes it easier to compare how your record moves. Some days flow; the next day the same stop blocks you. What matters is remembering the sticky stations and checking that stretch a little more carefully next round.
landing.seo.lines.title: Familiar subway lines, turned into a typing game
landing.seo.lines.p1: Play uses real subway lines and station order. Some stretches are short; others have many stops, so round length varies. Even on a route you ride daily, the next station may not come instantly — and that moment is oddly fun, because it shows where your mental map and fingers disagree.
landing.seo.lines.p2: Type correctly and the train moves forward; mistype and it stays put. Lower accuracy stretches your finish time too. It looks like a subway game, but inside it trains both line memory and typing. Knowing a station name is not the same as typing it immediately. The laggy stretches are where shaving your record begins.
landing.seo.lines.p3: After a few Metro Typing rounds, even familiar lines surprise you — especially near transfers or similar-sounding names. Slow down briefly, check the characters, then bring the tempo back. That is why a subway typing game also works as typing practice. Replay the same line right after finishing and feel how quickly the sticky station sticks. Lines differ in name length and difficulty; some feel easy, others stutter from the start — and noticing that difference is part of the fun.
landing.seo.tips.title: How to improve your Metro Typing record
landing.seo.tips.p1: Instead of starting on a long line, practice Metro Typing on a line you use often. Familiar order lets you prepare the next input so your hands stutter less. Build rhythm on a short stretch, then move to longer lines.
landing.seo.tips.p2: Prioritize accuracy over forcing speed. One typo becomes a stop that pushes the whole record later. Look at the keyboard less and repeat the same line so hand position settles. In subway typing, “don’t stop” beats “go fast.” Chasing the result numbers alone often means getting stuck at the same station again next round.
landing.seo.tips.p3: Check your Hangul input settings too. Autocomplete can insert the wrong characters and break flow. For Metro Typing, type in short bursts, recall sticky stations separately, then try again. Even if the record does not drop overnight, watch typos fall on the same line. Two or three sessions a week in the same direction can smooth early sticky stretches. Confirming Hangul input before practice alone cuts needless stops.
landing.seo.audience.title: Who will enjoy this game?
landing.seo.audience.p1: If you like subways and transit, testing your line memory is a good hook. If you want Hangul typing practice, this subway game gives meaningful words to warm up with — especially when random-word drills feel dull.
landing.seo.audience.p2: It also fits people who chase personal bests, or who know Korean subways well enough to guess the next stop. Compare finish times with friends on the same line. You do not need to be a fast typist; finishing a full loop with accuracy is the challenge.
landing.seo.audience.p3: One light Seoul Metro typing round often leads to trying the next line. Use it as a commute warm-up, or tackle a long line on the weekend so it does not get stale. If you came for “play now,” start in the frame above. Solo record hunting works; light contests on an agreed line work too.
landing.seo.faq.title: Frequently asked questions
landing.seo.faq.a1: Yes. Play in the browser with no payment. Pick a line and start.
landing.seo.faq.q2: Can I play without signing up?
landing.seo.faq.a2: Yes. No account needed — choose a line and start typing. Setup stays light.
landing.seo.faq.q3: Can I play the subway typing game on my phone?
landing.seo.faq.a3: It opens in mobile browsers. For fast station names, a physical keyboard is more comfortable. Short practice stretches still work on phone.
landing.seo.faq.q4: How is the Metro Typing record calculated?
landing.seo.faq.a4: You usually see finish time, typing speed, and accuracy together. More typos mean more stops and a longer total time. Repeating the same line makes progress easy to compare.
landing.seo.faq.q5: Can I play if my Hangul typing is slow?
landing.seo.faq.a5: Yes. Start with a familiar short line to build accuracy, then try longer stretches. For subway typing, learning the finish flow matters before raw speed.
